hooks_predicted: These audiences sit here from 7 in the morning all the way to 10 at night.
there are no tickets, anyone can watch for free
some people arrive 4 hours early, just to get a good spot
back in the 1930s, Charlie Chaplin visited China and watched traditional Chinese opera
Looks almost the same as what he saw nearly 100 years ago.
there are no doors on the toilets, people have to call out first to check if someone is inside before using them
preparing lunch for more than 50 members of the troupe
This troupe has built a following of nearly 1 million on the Chinese version of TikTok
there are more than 100 boxes of various sizes
each one can weigh anywhere from dozens to over 100 kilograms, which is roughly between 100 and over 200 pounds
The next destination is about 100 kilometers away, which is roughly 60 miles
For the next three months, this cycle continues.
selling_points_predicted: Unprecedented behind-the-scenes access showing what really happens with a traveling Chinese opera troupe over two full days
Witness performers working extreme 36-hour non-stop shifts, performing all day then traveling overnight to repeat the cycle
Free performances with no tickets or limits where audiences arrive 4 hours early and stay from 7am to 10pm
See traditional Chinese opera with stage design, costumes and props virtually unchanged from what Charlie Chaplin witnessed in the 1930s
Learn how traditional opera makeup reveals each character's morality and personality before a single word is spoken
Watch performers apply their own intricate traditional makeup without any professional makeup team
Experience the harsh reality of private opera troupes surviving without government funding in modern China
See how the troupe built nearly 1 million followers on Chinese TikTok to generate income through livestreaming
Witness the internal conflict between showing only performances versus revealing the struggles and daily hardships behind the scenes
Hear firsthand accounts of how COVID-19 pandemic devastated the traveling opera troupe's ability to survive
See the troupe's worn equipment, patched costumes, and even a drum with a hole taped up still being used in performances
Watch a mother perform on stage while other troupe members care for her baby backstage
Meet a sound technician with a physical disability who continues working on the front line traveling with the troupe
Experience the massive teardown operation where performers load over 100 boxes weighing 100-200 pounds each after every show
See the brutal performance schedule of 3 shows per day with relocation to a new city every 3 days for months on end
Witness the extreme living conditions from abandoned houses to sleeping in tents outdoors when accommodation isn't available
